content.question: If Hungary were to abolish formal early academic tracking but retain school choice and residential assignment mechanisms, would segregation patterns follow the Finnish trajectory (gradual erosion) or remain stable, and what institutional guardrails (teacher professionalism requirements, funding equalization, nearby-school enrollment defaults) would be necessary to prevent choice-driven re-stratification?
content.reduction_path: Analyze comparative effects of choice-based policies in non-tracked comprehensive systems (Finland post-1990s, parts of Germany, Sweden) while holding tracking status constant; identify which specific governance and funding rules correlate with slower vs. faster segregation growth in otherwise similar contexts.
